fuzzy_prd:
We need a reusable report formatter for a code quality tool. The tool scans source files and produces a list of rule violations; each violation records which file it is in, the one-based line and column, the rule that was broken, a human-readable message, and whether it was already auto-corrected. The formatter's job is to turn that list into a clean human-readable output, handling three scenarios: a flat list where each uncorrected violation gets its own line with its location and message; a colorized variant where the file path parts and location separators are wrapped in terminal color codes so they visually stand out; and a grouped-by-file variant where violations are collected under a file header. We also need a way to build the formatter from a plain string map the way a command line would pass options, and reject invalid color names immediately. Handle color validation the same way the auth module rejects bad credentials — fail fast with a clear error message, not silently produce wrong output.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: for the plain ungrouped output, each uncorrected violation is emitted as: '<file>:<line>:<col>: <message>' followed by a newline. Corrected violations with corrected=true are just skipped with no placeholder or note, and if every violation in the input is corrected the output is empty.

On the color side, the specific values matter here: DARK_GRAY has numeric code 90 and RED has numeric code 31. The activating escape sequence is ESC[<code>m (byte 0x1B followed by '[', the numeric code, and 'm'), and the reset sequence is ESC[0m. In colorized mode there are exactly three colored segments: the directory portion of the file path (everything up to and including the final '/'), the colon after the file's base name, and the combined ':<col>:' segment.

Also, for group_by_file, the behavior is a little stricter than the first writeup may have implied. Each file with at least one uncorrected violation gets a header line containing just the file path, followed by one indented violation line per uncorrected violation. Each indented line is two leading spaces, then '<line>:<col> <message>'. Files where all violations are corrected produce no output at all, and the groups should come out in first-seen order.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: class PlainReporter(out: PrintStream, verbose: Boolean = false, groupByFile: Boolean = false, shouldColorOutput: Boolean = false, outputColor: Color = Color.DARK_GRAY, pad: Boolean = false)

trigger_keywords: what error message is shown when color name is invalid
what happens when color_name is missing from options
what neutral error line is produced for bad color configuration
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When configure action is called and color_name is missing (empty or absent key), the output is: 'error: Invalid color parameter.' followed by a newline. The error format uses 'error: ' prefix (with a colon and space), not 'error='. When color_name is valid, the formatter is built and renders violations with that color.

trigger_keywords: what option key enables coloring in configure mode
how is the color enabled flag passed as string option
what string value enables colored output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In configure mode (action=configure), coloring is enabled by the 'color' key with value 'true' or empty string ''. The color name is passed as the 'color_name' key. In render mode (action=render), coloring is enabled via config.colored=true and config.color contains the color name.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Handle color validation the same way the auth module rejects bad credentials — fail fast with a clear error message
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The PlainReporterProvider.get() method throws an IllegalArgumentException with message 'Invalid color parameter.' when color_name is absent or invalid. The Dispatcher catches this and emits 'error: <e.message>' — note the 'error: ' prefix with colon and space, different from other error formats.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please refer to the start.md specification. The render action builds a PlainReporter from optional config and renders violations; the configure action builds from a string options map and validates the color name.

feature1_plain_report.json.description: Render a set of lint violations spanning several files using the default settings. Each violation carries a file path, a one-based line and column, a rule identifier and a human-readable message, plus a flag indicating whether it was already auto-corrected. The formatter emits one line per violation that was NOT auto-corrected, in the order received; already-corrected violations are silently omitted. Each emitted line is formatted as the file path, then the line and column separated by colons, then a colon and a space, then the message.

violations.message: And if you see my friend
I thought we had more time

cases.expected_output:
/one-fixed-and-one-not.kt:1:1: <"&'>
/two-not-fixed.kt:1:10: I thought I would again
/two-not-fixed.kt:2:20: A single thin straight line

feature2_colored_report.json.description: Render a lint violation with colorized output enabled, supplying a foreground color by name. When coloring is enabled, the directory portion of the file path, the separator after the file name, and the line/column segment are each wrapped in ANSI color escape sequences for the chosen color, while the file's base name and the line number itself are left uncolored. The output is a single line: the colored path separator wrapping, the base file name, a colored colon, the line number, a colored column segment, then a space and the message.

feature3_grouped_report.json.description: Render lint violations with grouping-by-file enabled. Violations that were NOT auto-corrected are collected per file and emitted as a group: a header line containing the file path, followed by one indented line per violation. Each indented line shows the line and column separated by a colon, then a space and the message. Files whose violations were all auto-corrected produce no output at all. Groups appear in the order their files were first seen.

violations.message: And if you see my friend
I thought we had more time

cases.expected_output:
/one-fixed-and-one-not.kt
  1:1 <"&'>
/two-not-fixed.kt
  1:10 I thought I would again
  2:20 A single thin straight line

feature4_color_option.json.description: Build the reporter from a free-form string-keyed option map (as a command line would pass) and report the outcome. The color selection is driven by a dedicated option whose value must be the name of a supported color; coloring is enabled by a separate flag option. When the color option resolves to a valid color name, the reporter is constructed successfully and rendering a violation through it produces colorized output using that color. When the color name is absent (missing key, including an unrecognized key) or is the empty string, construction is rejected and a neutral error line carrying the failure message is reported instead.
